nodes.context: Philadelphia-based African-American organized crime syndicate active from 1960s to early 1980s, involved in drug trafficking, extortion, murder, and other rackets.
Religious organization; many Black Mafia members converted to Islam, giving the group the nickname 'Muslim Mafia'.
Italian-American Mafia family in Philadelphia; don Angelo Bruno discouraged drug dealing but tolerated some business with black organized crime.
New York Mafia family that did business with the Black Mafia, supplying drugs.
Later iteration or splinter of the Black Mafia mentioned at the end of the text.
Jamaican posses that took over drug distribution in black neighborhoods of Philadelphia in the 1980s.
Italian-American Mafia organization used as a model by the JBM.
Philadelphia-based Italian-American Mafia family; allied with Black Mafia and cooperated with JBM.
Allied organization mentioned in the infobox as an ally of the Black Mafia.

edges.context: Many Black Mafia members converted to Islam, giving the group the nickname 'Muslim Mafia'.
Angelo Bruno turned a blind eye to Italian gangsters doing business with black drug lords, with a rough territorial division east/west of Broad Street.
The Gambino family did business with the Black Mafia, supplying drugs through black New York drug lord Frank Matthews.
The Junior Black Mafia is a later iteration or splinter of the original Black Mafia.
Black Mafia members organized and mentored the JBM to counter Jamaican posses.
JBM was involved in a drug war with the Shower Posse in Southwest Philadelphia during the 1980s and early 1990s.
JBM modeled its criminal methods after La Cosa Nostra.
JBM cooperated with associates of the Bruno-Scarfo crime family in cocaine distribution.
Infobo lists the Philadelphia crime family as an ally of the Black Mafia.
Many Black Mafia members were Nation of Islam members, giving the group the nickname 'Muslim Mafia'.
Infobo lists The Council as an ally of the Black Mafia.
Infobo lists Shower Posse as a rival of the Black Mafia.
